skill.name: obsidian-cli
skill.description: Interact with Obsidian vaults using the Obsidian CLI to read, create, search, and manage notes, tasks, properties, and developer diagnostics.
skill.summary: Manage Obsidian vault content and developer workflows through the Obsidian CLI.
skill.author: kepano

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command hits because SKILL.md documents Obsidian CLI commands in Markdown backticks. Most hits are false positives for shell execution, but the skill intentionally instructs agents to run local CLI commands that can read, write, search, and inspect an Obsidian vault. The most important confirmed concern is the documented obsidian eval command, which runs JavaScript in the Obsidian app context.

medium_findings.title: Local Obsidian Vault Read and Write Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Obsidian CLI commands that can read notes, create notes, append content, set properties, list tags, and inspect backlinks. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can expose or modify private vault data if used without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands directly target vault content and include read, create, append, search, and property operations. The behavior is expected for this skill, but the data access and modification risk is real.

medium_findings.title: JavaScript Execution in Obsidian App Context
medium_findings.description: The skill documents obsidian eval for running JavaScript inside the Obsidian application context. This enables powerful plugin development workflows, but arbitrary JavaScript could access app data or alter application state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly labels this as running JavaScript in the app context and provides an obsidian eval command. This is a confirmed powerful execution capability, even though no malicious intent is present.

medium_findings.title: Developer Inspection Commands Can Reveal Local App Data
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends commands for screenshots, DOM inspection, console logs, errors, and CSS inspection. These commands support debugging, but they may capture private note content or workspace details from a running Obsidian instance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented for debugging and visual verification. The privacy risk is contextual because exposure depends on the user's open vault and workspace state.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged many Markdown inline code and fenced command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md is documentation, not executable Ruby code, so these specific pattern matches are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the flagged text is displayed command documentation. No Ruby source file or executable backtick expression exists in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URL
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to Obsidian CLI documentation. It is not used by executable code and does not send local data to a remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is presented as documentation text beside an obsidian help instruction. No network request or data transfer logic is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the front matter description line. No cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, or password handling is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is descriptive front matter for Obsidian vault operations. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic usage in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Obsidian CLI Command Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on local obsidian commands that can interact with the user's active vault and running Obsidian instance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation repeatedly directs the agent to invoke the obsidian CLI. This is intentional but creates local data access and mutation risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Application-Context JavaScript Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented obsidian eval command can execute JavaScript against the running Obsidian app.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly says to run JavaScript in the app context and gives an eval example. This confirms a powerful execution surface.

content.user_title: Manage Obsidian Notes from the CLI
content.value_statement: Obsidian vault work can be slow when every action requires manual navigation. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use the Obsidian CLI for notes, tasks, search, and plugin debugging.
actual_capabilities: Read, create, append, and search notes in an open Obsidian vault.
Target notes by wikilink-style file name or exact vault-relative path.
Manage daily notes, tasks, tags, backlinks, and frontmatter properties.
Reload Obsidian plugins and inspect app errors during development.
Capture screenshots, inspect DOM content, and review console output.
Run JavaScript in Obsidian for advanced plugin and theme checks.
limitations: Requires Obsidian to be open with the CLI available.
Targets the most recently focused vault unless a vault name is provided.
Can expose or modify private vault content through local commands.
Advanced developer commands require careful user confirmation.

use_cases.title: Maintain Personal Notes
use_cases.description: Create notes, append updates, search content, and manage properties without leaving a coding assistant session.

use_cases.title: Review Tasks and Daily Notes
use_cases.description: Read daily notes, add tasks, list todos, and summarize vault work from command output.

use_cases.title: Debug Obsidian Plugins
use_cases.description: Reload plugins, collect app errors, inspect the DOM, and capture screenshots during plugin development.

prompt_templates.title: Read a Note
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Obsidian CLI to read the note named Project Plan and summarize its main action items.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Structured Note
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Obsidian note named Meeting Notes with headings for attendees, decisions, and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Update Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Read today's daily note, append a new unchecked task, and then show the updated task list.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Reload the Obsidian plugin my-plugin, check errors, inspect the workspace DOM, and report likely fixes.

output_examples.input: Search my vault for launch checklist and show the most relevant notes.
output_examples.output: A short ranked list of matching notes with titles, paths, and brief context from each result.

output_examples.input: Add a task to today's daily note and confirm it was saved.
output_examples.output: Confirmation that the task was appended, followed by the updated daily task section.

output_examples.input: Reload my plugin and check whether Obsidian reported errors.
output_examples.output: A concise debugging report with reload status, observed errors, console messages, and suggested next steps.

best_practices: Ask the user before creating, overwriting, appending, or changing vault content.
Use exact vault names and paths when multiple vaults or duplicate note names may exist.
Treat screenshots, DOM output, console logs, and eval results as potentially private data.
anti_patterns: Do not run obsidian eval with untrusted JavaScript or unclear user intent.
Do not use overwrite commands without confirming the target note and expected result.
Do not assume the active vault is correct when the user mentions a specific vault.

faq.question: Does this skill require Obsidian to be open?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented commands interact with a running Obsidian instance through the Obsidian CLI.

faq.question: Can it edit my notes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can create notes, append content, set properties, and perform other vault operations when requested.

faq.question: Can it work with more than one vault?
faq.answer: Yes. Commands can include a vault name, but the default target is the most recently focused vault.

faq.question: Is this safe for private vaults?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can read and expose note content through command output, screenshots, and diagnostics.

faq.question: What development workflows does it support?
faq.answer: It supports plugin reloads, error checks, console review, screenshots, DOM inspection, CSS inspection, and JavaScript evaluation.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it provides local vault access, note modification, and JavaScript execution in Obsidian.
